metadata.notes: Tests for Bayesian reasoning, conjunction fallacy, base-rate neglect, Monty Hall (but never the canonical 3-door form), birthday paradox, two-envelope, and gambler's fallacy. Linda-style stems use varied names and details to defeat rote pattern matching.

stimuli.prompt: A game show has 5 doors. Behind one is a prize; behind the other four are goats. You pick door 2. The host, who knows where the prize is, opens three of the other doors (1, 3, 5), all revealing goats. He offers you a chance to switch to door 4. What is your probability of winning if you switch vs. if you stay?
stimuli.expected: Switching: 4/5 = 80%. Staying: 1/5 = 20%.

stimuli.prompt: Modified Monty Hall: 7 doors. You pick door A. The host opens five other doors revealing goats, leaving your door A and one other (door B). Should you switch to B? What's the probability of winning if you switch?
stimuli.expected: Switch. P(win | switch) = 6/7 ≈ 85.7%. P(win | stay) = 1/7 ≈ 14.3%.

stimuli.prompt: Variant: 5 doors. You pick door 3. The host opens just ONE other door (door 1) revealing a goat, then offers a switch from door 3 to any remaining unopened door (2, 4, or 5). Should you switch, and to what probability?
stimuli.expected: Switching to any single specified remaining door: each has probability 4/15. Staying: 1/5 = 3/15. So switch (any of the three is better than staying). P(win|stay)=1/5; P(win|switch to one specific door)=4/15.

stimuli.prompt: 5-door Monty: You pick door 1. The host opens doors 2 and 3, both goats. (He has not opened all the others — doors 4 and 5 remain.) Should you switch from door 1, and if so, to which door is best?
stimuli.expected: Switch. P(win|stay on door 1) = 1/5. P(win on door 4) = 2/5. P(win on door 5) = 2/5. Either remaining door doubles your chance.

stimuli.prompt: 7 doors. You pick door C. The host opens four other doors revealing goats — three doors remain unopened including yours. The host offers a switch. What is P(win) if you switch to a specific other unopened door?
stimuli.expected: Stay: 1/7. Each of the two remaining unopened doors not your own: 3/7 each. Switch.

stimuli.prompt: Marisol is 31 years old, single, outspoken, and very bright. She majored in environmental engineering. As a student she protested fossil-fuel projects and worked on a campaign for safe drinking water. Which is more probable? (a) Marisol is an engineer. (b) Marisol is an engineer who volunteers for environmental causes.
stimuli.expected: (a) — the conjunction (b) cannot be more probable than its component (a). Conjunction fallacy.

stimuli.prompt: Tariq is 45, was a competitive chess player as a teenager, and studied mathematics. He plays poker on weekends and reads books on probability for fun. Which is more probable? (a) Tariq works in finance. (b) Tariq works in finance and plays in amateur chess tournaments.
stimuli.expected: (a) — conjunction must be ≤ component.

stimuli.prompt: Aiko is 28, a graduate of a prestigious art school. She paints murals and lives in a co-op. Which is more probable? (a) Aiko works as a barista. (b) Aiko works as a barista and is a member of an artists' collective.

Soo-min is 36, a former Olympic swimmer who studied physical therapy. Which is more probable? (a) Soo-min works in healthcare. (b) Soo-min works in healthcare AND coaches a youth swimming team. (c) Soo-min works in tech.
stimuli.expected: (a) is the most probable; (b) is a strict subset of (a) and cannot exceed it.

stimuli.prompt: In a city, 85% of taxis are green and 15% are blue. A witness reports a hit-and-run by a blue taxi. Tested under similar conditions, the witness correctly identifies cab color 80% of the time. What is the probability the cab was actually blue, given the witness's report?

A disease affects 1 in 1000 people. A test for the disease has 99% sensitivity (true positive rate) and 99% specificity (true negative rate). A randomly tested person tests positive. What is the probability they have the disease?
Variant: A rare neurological disorder affects 1 in 10,000 people. A new screening test has 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity. If you test positive, what is the probability you have the disorder?

A police lineup has 6 people. The witness identifies one. The witness is right 70% of the time when shown the actual perpetrator and chooses uniformly among the others when wrong. The actual perpetrator was in the lineup with prior probability 50%. What is P(identified person is the perpetrator | identification)?
How many people must be in a room before there is at least a 50% chance that two share a birthday (ignoring leap years)?
Variant: In a room of 70 people, what is the approximate probability that at least two share a birthday?
stimuli.expected: About 99.9% (essentially certain).

stimuli.prompt: Variant: How many people are needed for a 50% chance that two were born in the same month (ignoring days)?
stimuli.expected: 4 people. P(no match with k people, 12 months) drops below 0.5 between k=4 and k=5; at k=4, P(all distinct) = 12/12 × 11/12 × 10/12 × 9/12 ≈ 0.572, so P(match) ≈ 0.428. At k=5, P(all distinct) ≈ 0.382, P(match) ≈ 0.618. So 5 people gives >50%.

stimuli.prompt: Two-envelope puzzle: Two envelopes contain money — one has twice as much as the other. You pick one and find $100 inside. The other contains either $50 or $200. The 'expected value' argument says: if you switch, you get (0.5)(50) + (0.5)(200) = $125, more than $100. Should you switch? Why might this argument be wrong?
stimuli.expected: The naive expected-value argument misuses probabilities — it assumes a uniform prior over the unknown amounts, which leads to paradox. Without a proper prior on possible amounts, both envelopes are symmetric: there is no advantage to switching. Switching ad infinitum (doing this with the other envelope first) leads to contradiction.

stimuli.prompt: A sealed box contains $X; another contains $2X. You open the first and see $80. The 'switch' argument: half the time the other has $40, half the time $160 — average $100, switch! Then by symmetry the same argument applies after switching. Resolve.
stimuli.expected: The argument secretly assumes a uniform improper prior on X. With any proper prior (e.g., bounded above by some maximum), the calculation changes — at high amounts, the conditional probability that the other envelope is larger drops below 50%. Symmetry of the setup means no a priori advantage to either choice.

stimuli.prompt: A fair coin has come up heads ten times in a row. What is the probability the next flip is tails?
stimuli.expected: 0.5 (50%). Coin flips are independent.

stimuli.prompt: A roulette wheel (without 0) has hit black 8 times in a row. Bystanders bet heavily on red, saying it is 'overdue'. Are they right?
stimuli.expected: No. Each spin is independent; previous outcomes do not affect the next. P(red on next spin) = 0.5.

stimuli.prompt: A couple has had four daughters in a row. They want a son and conclude that 'the next one is bound to be a boy.' Is this reasoning correct?
stimuli.expected: No. Each birth is approximately independent (about 51/49 boy/girl); previous births don't change the next.

stimuli.prompt: Inverse: A fair coin has come up heads 10 times in a row. Some argue: 'It must be biased — bet on heads next.' Is this reasoning ever correct?
stimuli.expected: Yes, in a Bayesian sense — observing 10 heads in a row should slightly raise the posterior that the coin is biased toward heads, IF you had any prior probability mass on biased coins. This is the 'hot hand' counterpart to the gambler's fallacy. With a strict prior of 'definitely fair' it remains 50/50.

stimuli.prompt: A lottery uses 6 numbers from 1-49. A common belief: numbers that haven't won recently are 'due'. Should you pick rarely-drawn numbers?
stimuli.expected: It does not increase your chance of winning (draws are independent). However, picking unpopular numbers may increase your expected payout if you DO win, because you are less likely to share the prize.

stimuli.prompt: An algorithm flags credit-card transactions as fraud. Of all transactions, 0.2% are actually fraud. The algorithm has 98% sensitivity and 97% specificity. Given a flagged transaction, what is P(fraud)?

Felipe is 52, used to play in a punk band, has multiple tattoos, and rides a motorcycle. He now teaches high-school history. Which is more probable? (a) Felipe rides his motorcycle to work. (b) Felipe rides his motorcycle to work AND was once arrested at a protest concert.
stimuli.expected: (a) — conjunction cannot exceed component.

stimuli.prompt: 5-door Monty: You pick door 4. The host opens doors 1 and 2 (both goats). Doors 3, 4, 5 remain unopened. The host offers a switch. Should you switch, and to which? What is P(win) if you switch to door 3?
stimuli.expected: Stay on door 4: 1/5 = 20%. Switch to door 3: 2/5 = 40%. Switch to door 5: 2/5 = 40%. Either remaining door is twice as good as staying.

stimuli.prompt: Variant: How many people are needed for a 50% chance that two share a birth-DAY-OF-WEEK (only 7 categories)?
stimuli.expected: 4 people. P(no shared day-of-week with 4 people) = 7/7 × 6/7 × 5/7 × 4/7 = 120/343 ≈ 0.350; so P(at least one match) ≈ 0.650 > 0.5. With 3 people: P(no match) = 7/7 × 6/7 × 5/7 = 30/49 ≈ 0.612, P(match) ≈ 0.388 < 0.5. So 4.

stimuli.prompt: Hot-hand variant: A basketball player has made her last six free throws in a row. A teammate says 'pass to her, she's hot.' Skeptic says 'no, this is gambler's fallacy.' Who is right?
stimuli.expected: Recent research suggests the hot hand is real to a small degree (player skill, motor consistency, defender adaptation). It is not pure gambler's fallacy. The teammate's intuition has some basis; the skeptic is overconfident in the symmetric independence model.

stimuli.prompt: A new home pregnancy test claims '99% accurate'. Among women who suspect pregnancy enough to test, prior probability of actual pregnancy is about 50%. With 99% sensitivity and 99% specificity, what is P(pregnant | positive)?
stimuli.expected: P = (0.5 × 0.99) / (0.5 × 0.99 + 0.5 × 0.01) = 0.495 / 0.5 = 0.99. About 99%. (When base rate is 50%, accuracy headlines do match the posterior.)

stimuli.prompt: Naledi grew up on a farm, has competed in horse-riding events since age 7, and majored in veterinary science. Which is more probable? (a) Naledi works with animals professionally. (b) Naledi works with animals professionally AND owns at least one horse.
stimuli.expected: (a) — conjunction must be ≤ component.
